question: Define a first-order moving average process, MA(1). Write out its equation and explain the role of the coefficient θ.
expectedPoints: The MA(1) process is defined as X_t = μ + ε_t + θε_{t-1}.
The term ε_t represents a white noise error term at time t.
The coefficient θ determines the magnitude and direction of the influence of the previous period's shock (ε_{t-1}) on the current value (X_t).

question: Describe the key properties of a moving average process of order q, MA(q). Your answer should specifically cover its mean, variance, autocorrelation function (ACF), and stationarity.
expectedPoints: An MA(q) process is always stationary, as it is a finite linear combination of white noise terms.
The mean of the process is constant and equal to μ.
The variance is also constant and can be derived as Var(X_t) = σ²(1 + θ₁² + θ₂² + ... + θ_q²).
The autocorrelation function (ACF) cuts off abruptly after lag q. This is a defining characteristic used to identify the order of an MA process.

question: Compare and contrast a Moving Average (MA) process with an Autoregressive (AR) process. Your discussion should explain the fundamental conceptual difference, the behavior of their Autocorrelation Function (ACF) and Partial Autocorrelation Function (PACF), and the implications for modeling a real-world time series.
expectedPoints: Conceptual Difference: An MA process expresses the current value as a linear combination of past shocks (white noise terms), while an AR process expresses it as a linear combination of past values of the series itself.

ACF/PACF Behavior: The ACF of an MA(q) process cuts off after lag q, while its PACF tails off. Conversely, the PACF of an AR(p) process cuts off after lag p, while its ACF tails off. This inverse relationship is crucial for model identification.
Invertibility: An MA process is invertible (can be represented as an AR(∞)) if its roots lie outside the unit circle, a condition analogous to the stationarity condition for an AR process.

Modeling Implications: The choice between an AR or MA model is guided by the observed ACF and PACF. A series with significant autocorrelations for only a few lags suggests an MA model, while one with a slowly decaying ACF suggests an AR model.
